revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''パヨカカムイ'''は、[[アイヌ]]に伝わる[[疫病]]の神。 アイヌの神にしては珍しく、人々に害をもたらすのが役目とされ...'
revision.text:
'''パヨカカムイ'''は、[[アイヌ]]に伝わる[[疫病]]の神。 アイヌの神にしては珍しく、人々に害をもたらすのが役目とされる。
とある村のはずれに、貧しいアイヌの一家が住んでいた。そこの父親は狩りが下手であったが、[[ユーカラ]]（民話詩）を語るのは上手だった。ある時、その父親が子供達にユーカラを語っていると、家の外にパヨカカムイがいる事に気づいた。そこで捧げ物をすると、パヨカカムイは夢枕に現れ、上手なユーカラと捧げ物の礼をいい、病を避ける方法を伝えて去っていった。この方法を村長に伝えた所、村長は感謝し、村の者は皆疫病に冒されること無く、幸せに暮らしたとされる。
== 参考文献 == * 『悪魔事典』 [[新紀元社]]、2000年、232~233頁。
{{DEFAULTSORT:はよかかむい}} [[Category:アイヌ]] [[Category:アイヌの神]]

'''パヨカカムイ'''は、[[アイヌ]]に伝わる[[疫病]]の神。 アイヌの神にしては珍しく、人々に害をもたらすのが役目とされる。
とある村のはずれに、貧しいアイヌの一家が住んでいた。そこの父親は狩りが下手であったが、[[ユーカラ]]（民話詩）を語るのは上手だった。ある時、その父親が子供達にユーカラを語っていると、家の外にパヨカカムイがいる事に気づいた。そこで捧げ物をすると、パヨカカムイは夢枕に現れ、上手なユーカラと捧げ物の礼をいい、病を避ける方法を伝えて去っていった。この方法を村長に伝えた所、村長は感謝し、村の者は皆疫病に冒されること無く、幸せに暮らしたとされる。
== 参考文献 == * 『悪魔事典』 [[新紀元社]]、2000年、232~233頁。
{{DEFAULTSORT:はよかかむい}} [[Category:アイヌの伝承]] [[Category:アイヌの神]]

'''パヨカカムイ'''は、[[アイヌ]]に伝わる[[疫病]]の神。 アイヌの神にしては珍しく、人々に害をもたらすのが役目とされる。
とある村のはずれに、貧しいアイヌの一家が住んでいた。そこの父親は狩りが下手であったが、[[ユーカラ]]（民話詩）を語るのは上手だった。ある時、その父親が子供達にユーカラを語っていると、家の外にパヨカカムイがいる事に気づいた。そこで捧げ物をすると、パヨカカムイは夢枕に現れ、上手なユーカラと捧げ物の礼をいい、病を避ける方法を伝えて去っていった。この方法を村長に伝えた所、村長は感謝し、村の者は皆疫病に冒されること無く、幸せに暮らしたとされる。

revision.comment: ロボットによる 追加: [[ko:파요카카무이]]
revision.text:
'''パヨカカムイ'''または'''パイカイカムイ'''は、[[アイヌ]]に伝わる[[疫病]]の[[神]]<ref name="murakami">[[村上健司]]編著 『妖怪事典』 [[毎日新聞社]]、2000年、267-268頁。ISBN 978-4-6203-1428-0。</ref>。
アイヌの神にしては珍しく、人々に害をもたらすのが役目とされる<ref name="akuma">[[山北篤]]・佐藤俊之監修 『悪魔事典』 [[新紀元社]]〈Truth In Fantasy〉、2000年、232-233頁。ISBN 978-4-88317-353-2。</ref>。[[天然痘|疱瘡（天然痘）]]や流行病を司る神であり、姿を見せずに弓を放ち、この矢を射る音を聞いた者は疱瘡に侵されるといわれる<ref name="murakami" />。
とある村のはずれに、貧しいアイヌの一家が住んでいた。そこの父親は狩りが下手であったが、[[ユーカラ]]（民話詩）を語るのは上手だった。ある時、その父親が子供達にユーカラを語っていると、家の外にパヨカカムイがいることに気づいた。そこで捧げ物をすると、パヨカカムイは夢枕に現れ、上手なユーカラと捧げ物の礼をいい、病を避ける方法を伝えて去っていった。この方法を村長に伝えたところ、村長は感謝し、村の者は皆疫病に冒されること無く、幸せに暮らしたとされる<ref name="akuma" />。
また、このパヨカカムイと人間との間には'''カスンテ'''という怪人物が生まれた。カスンテは何度殺されても生まれ変わったが、[[厚岸郡|厚岸]]のアイヌたちが上下の顎を別々にすることで生まれ変わることはなくなった。しかし、そのアイヌたちの多くは疱瘡を煩って死に、わずかに残った者たちも[[津波]]に遭って死んだ。そのために津波はカスンテの祟りといわれたという<ref>『妖怪事典』 105頁。</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:はよかかむい}} [[Category:アイヌの伝承]] [[Category:アイヌの神]]
[[ko:파요카카무이]]

'''パヨカカムイ'''または'''パイカイカムイ'''は、[[アイヌ]]に伝わる[[疫病]]の[[神]]<ref name="murakami">[[村上健司]]編著 『妖怪事典』 [[毎日新聞社]]、2000年、267-268頁。ISBN 978-4-6203-1428-0。</ref>。

また、このパヨカカムイと人間との間には'''カスンテ'''という怪人物が生まれた。カスンテは何度殺されても生まれ変わったが、[[厚岸郡|厚岸]]のアイヌたちが上下の顎を別々にすることで生まれ変わることはなくなった。しかし、そのアイヌたちの多くは疱瘡を煩って死に、わずかに残った者たちも[[津波]]に遭って死んだ。そのために津波はカスンテの祟りといわれたという<ref>『妖怪事典』 105頁。</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:はよかかむい}} [[Category:アイヌの伝承]] [[Category:アイヌの神]] [[Category:天然痘]]

{{DEFAULTSORT:はよかかむい}} [[Category:アイヌの伝承]] [[Category:アイヌの神]] [[Category:天然痘]]

'''パヨカカムイ'''または'''パイカイカムイ'''は、[[アイヌ]]に伝わる[[疫病]]の[[神]]<ref name="murakami">[[村上健司]]編著 『妖怪事典』 [[毎日新聞社]]、2000年、267-268頁。ISBN 978-4-6203-1428-0。</ref>。
アイヌの神にしては珍しく、人々に害をもたらすのが役目とされる<ref name="akuma">[[山北篤]]・佐藤俊之監修 『悪魔事典』 [[新紀元社]]〈Truth In Fantasy〉、2000年、232-233頁。ISBN 978-4-88317-353-2。</ref>。[[天然痘|疱瘡（天然痘）]]や流行病を司る神であり、姿を見せずに弓を放ち、この矢を射る音を聞いた者は疱瘡に侵されるといわれる<ref name="murakami" />。
とある村のはずれに、貧しいアイヌの一家が住んでいた。そこの父親は狩りが下手であったが、[[ユーカラ]]（民話詩）を語るのは上手だった。ある時、その父親が子供達にユーカラを語っていると、家の外にパヨカカムイがいることに気づいた。そこで捧げ物をすると、パヨカカムイは夢枕に現れ、上手なユーカラと捧げ物の礼をいい、病を避ける方法を伝えて去っていった。この方法を村長に伝えたところ、村長は感謝し、村の者は皆疫病に冒されること無く、幸せに暮らしたとされる<ref name="akuma" />。この言い伝えを元に疫病が流行した際には、パヨカカムイに疫病を終息を願う儀式が行われるようになった<ref>[https://this.kiji.is/624148580355900513 「パヨカカムイ」、近づかないで アイヌ民族が祈りの儀式] - [[共同通信]]</ref>。
また、このパヨカカムイと人間との間には'''カスンテ'''という怪人物が生まれた。カスンテは何度殺されても生まれ変わったが、[[厚岸郡|厚岸]]のアイヌたちが上下の顎を別々にすることで生まれ変わることはなくなった。しかし、そのアイヌたちの多くは疱瘡を煩って死に、わずかに残った者たちも[[津波]]に遭って死んだ。そのために津波はカスンテの祟りといわれたという<ref>『妖怪事典』 105頁。</ref>。

'''Payokakamuy''' (パヨカカムイ, Payokakamuy) or '''Pay''' (パイカイカムイ, Pai Kaimamuye) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神|lt=deity}} of {{ill|plague|ja|疫病|lt=plague}} appearing in {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ|lt=Ainu}} folklore.<ref name="murakami">{{ill|Murakami Kenji|ja|村上健司|lt=Murakami Kenji}} (ed.), 『Yokai Encyclopedia』, [[Mainichi Newspapers]] Publishing, 2000, pp. 267-268. ISBN 978-4-6203-1428-0.</ref>
Unlike most {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ|lt=Ainu}} deities, Payokakamuy is uniquely tasked with bringing harm to people.<ref name="akuma">{{ill|Yamakita Atsushi|ja|山北篤|lt=Yamakita Atsushi}} & {{ill|Sato Toshiyuki|ja|佐藤俊之|lt=Sato Toshiyuki}} (eds.), 『Akuma Encyclopedia』, [[Shin-kiyoosha]]〈Truth In Fantasy〉, 2000, pp. 232-233. ISBN 978-4-88317-353-2.</ref> He governs diseases such as {{ill|smallpox|ja|疱瘡（天然痘）|lt=smallpox}} and epidemics. Payokakamuy does not reveal his form; instead, he releases arrows by shooting bows. Those who hear the sound of his arrows being shot are said to be afflicted with smallpox.<ref name="murakami" />
A poor Ainu family lived on the outskirts of a village. The father was poor at hunting but was skilled at narrating {{ill|Yukar|ja|ユーカラ|lt=Yukar}} (Ainu folk songs). One day, while the father was telling Yukar to his children, he noticed Payokakamuy outside their home. Upon offering a sacrifice, Payokakamuy appeared in the woman's dream, praising her Yukar skills and the sacrificial offerings, and then conveyed a method to prevent illness before departing. When the woman shared this method with the village chief, the chief expressed gratitude, and the villagers lived happily without succumbing to plagues.<ref name="akuma" /> Based on this legend, when an epidemic breaks out, rituals are performed to beseech Payokakamuy to end the disease.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20200418125506/https://this.kiji.is/624148580355900513 「パヨカカムイ」、近づかないで アイヌ民族が祈りの儀式] - [[Kyodo News]]</ref>
Additionally, a monstrous figure named '''Kasunte''' was born from the relationship between Payokakamuy and humans. Kasunte was resurrected each time he was killed, but the Ainu of {{ill|Abashiri|ja|厚岸郡|lt=Abashiri}} prevented his reincarnation by separating his upper and lower jaws. However, many of these Ainu perished from smallpox, and the few remaining were killed by {{ill|tsunami|ja|津波|lt=tsunami}}, leading to the belief that tsunamis were the vengeances of Kasunte.<ref>{{ill|Yokai Encyclopedia|ja|『妖怪事典』|lt=Yokai Encyclopedia}}, p. 105.</ref>
== References == * 松谷みよ子『日本の伝説（下）』[[Kodansha]] Bunko, 1975, pp. 259-265. * {{ill|Murakami Kenji|ja|村上健司|lt=Murakami Kenji}} (ed.), 『Yokai Encyclopedia』, [[Mainichi Newspapers]] Publishing, 2000, pp. 267-268. ISBN 978-4-6203-1428-0. * {{ill|Yamakita Atsushi|ja|山北篤|lt=Yamakita Atsushi}} & {{ill|Sato Toshiyuki|ja|佐藤俊之|lt=Sato Toshiyuki}} (eds.), 『Akuma Encyclopedia』, [[Shin-kiyoosha]]〈Truth In Fantasy〉, 2000, pp. 232-233. ISBN 978-4-88317-353-2.
== Related Topics == * [[List of Japanese Yokai]] * [[Lat Shikamuye]] * [[Ipetamu]] – A legend states that this sword was used to defeat Payokakamuy.
{{DEFAULTSORT:はよかかむい}} [[Category:Ainu Folklore]] [[Category:Ainu Deities]] [[Category:Smallpox]] [[Category:Original Pages]] [[ja:パヨカカムイ]]

'''Payokakamuy''' (パヨカカムイ, Payokakamuy) or '''Pay''' (パイカイカムイ, Pai Kaimamuye) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神|lt=deity}} of {{ill|plague|ja|疫病|lt=plague}} appearing in {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ|lt=Ainu}} folklore.<ref name="murakami">{{ill|Murakami Kenji|ja|村上健司|lt=Murakami Kenji}} (ed.), 『Yokai Encyclopedia』, [[Mainichi Newspapers]] Publishing, 2000, pp. 267-268. ISBN 978-4-6203-1428-0.</ref>
Unlike most {{ill|Ainu|ja|アイヌ|lt=Ainu}} deities, Payokakamuy is uniquely tasked with bringing harm to people.<ref name="akuma">{{ill|Yamakita Atsushi|ja|山北篤|lt=Yamakita Atsushi}} & {{ill|Sato Toshiyuki|ja|佐藤俊之|lt=Sato Toshiyuki}} (eds.), 『Akuma Encyclopedia』, [[Shin-kiyoosha]]〈Truth In Fantasy〉, 2000, pp. 232-233. ISBN 978-4-88317-353-2.</ref> He governs diseases such as {{ill|smallpox|ja|疱瘡（天然痘）|lt=smallpox}} and epidemics. Payokakamuy does not reveal his form; instead, he releases arrows by shooting bows. Those who hear the sound of his arrows being shot are said to be afflicted with smallpox.<ref name="murakami" />
A poor Ainu family lived on the outskirts of a village. The father was poor at hunting but was skilled at narrating {{ill|Yukar|ja|ユーカラ|lt=Yukar}} (Ainu folk songs). One day, while the father was telling Yukar to his children, he noticed Payokakamuy outside their home. Upon offering a sacrifice, Payokakamuy appeared in the woman's dream, praising her Yukar skills and the sacrificial offerings, and then conveyed a method to prevent illness before departing. When the woman shared this method with the village chief, the chief expressed gratitude, and the villagers lived happily without succumbing to plagues.<ref name="akuma" /> Based on this legend, when an epidemic breaks out, rituals are performed to beseech Payokakamuy to end the disease.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20200418125506/https://this.kiji.is/624148580355900513 「パヨカカムイ」、近づかないで アイヌ民族が祈りの儀式] - [[Kyodo News]]</ref>
Additionally, a monstrous figure named '''Kasunte''' was born from the relationship between Payokakamuy and humans. Kasunte was resurrected each time he was killed, but the Ainu of {{ill|Abashiri|ja|厚岸郡|lt=Abashiri}} prevented his reincarnation by separating his upper and lower jaws. However, many of these Ainu perished from smallpox, and the few remaining were killed by {{ill|tsunami|ja|津波|lt=tsunami}}, leading to the belief that tsunamis were the vengeances of Kasunte.<ref>{{ill|Yokai Encyclopedia|ja|『妖怪事典』|lt=Yokai Encyclopedia}}, p. 105.</ref>

{{DEFAULTSORT:はよかかむい}} [[Category:Ainu Folklore]] [[Category:Ainu Deities]] [[Category:Smallpox]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]]{{defaultsort:Payokakamuy}}[[category:original pages]] [[ja:パヨカカムイ]]

'''Payokakamu'i''' (パヨカカムイ) or '''Paikaikamu'i''' (パイカイカムイ) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} of {{ill|epidemics|ja|疫病}} in {{ill|Ainu mythology|ja|アイヌ}}.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Kenji Murakami|ja|村上健司}} | title=妖怪事典 | publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] | year=2000 | pages=267-268 | isbn=978-4-6203-1428-0}}</ref>
Unlike most Ainu deities, Payokakamu'i is known for bringing harm to people.<ref name="akuma">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Atsushi Yamakita|ja|山北篤}}, supervised by Sato Toshiyuki | title=悪魔事典 | publisher=[[Shinkigensha]] | series=Truth In Fantasy | year=2000 | pages=232-233 | isbn=978-4-88317-353-2}}</ref> The deity governs {{ill|smallpox|ja|天然痘}} and other contagious diseases. It is said to shoot invisible arrows, and those who hear the sound of these arrows are afflicted with smallpox.<ref name="murakami" />
According to one tale, a poor Ainu family lived on the outskirts of a village. The father was a poor hunter but a talented storyteller of {{ill|yukar|ja|ユーカラ}}, traditional narrative songs. One day, while telling stories to his children, the father realized Payokakamu'i was outside their home. Offering a sacrifice, the deity appeared in his dreams, expressing gratitude for the offering and the stories, and revealed a method to avoid illness before leaving. When the father shared this method with the village chief, the village expressed gratitude, and the villagers were spared from the epidemic, living happily ever after.<ref name="akuma" /> Inspired by this story, rituals were performed to pray to Payokakamu'i for the end of epidemics during outbreaks.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20200418125506/https://this.kiji.is/624148580355900513 "Payokakamu'i," Stay Away: Ainu Perform Prayer Rituals] - {{ill|Kyodo News|ja|共同通信}}</ref>
A mysterious figure called '''Kasunte''' was said to have been born between Payokakamu'i and a human. Kasunte repeatedly reincarnated after being killed, but the Ainu of {{ill|Akkeshi District|ja|厚岸郡}} finally ended this cycle by separating its upper and lower jaws. However, many of these Ainu succumbed to smallpox, and those who survived were later killed by a {{ill|tsunami|ja|津波}}. This led to the belief that the tsunami was Kasunte's curse.<ref>妖怪事典, p. 105.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * {{ill|Mintuci|ja|ミントゥチ}}

When the father shared this method with the village chief, the village expressed gratitude, and the villagers were spared from the epidemic, living happily ever after.<ref name="akuma" /> Inspired by this story, rituals were performed to pray to Payokakamu'i for the end of epidemics during outbreaks.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20200418125506/https://this.kiji.is/624148580355900513 "Payokakamu'i," Stay Away: Ainu Perform Prayer Rituals] - {{ill|Kyodo News|ja|共同通信}}</ref>
A mysterious figure called '''Kasunte''' was said to have been born between Payokakamu'i and a human. Kasunte repeatedly reincarnated after being killed, but the Ainu of {{ill|Akkeshi District|ja|厚岸郡}} finally ended this cycle by separating its upper and lower jaws. However, many of these Ainu succumbed to smallpox, and those who survived were later killed by a {{ill|tsunami|ja|津波}}. This led to the belief that the tsunami was Kasunte's curse.<ref>妖怪事典, p. 105.</ref>

Unlike most Ainu deities, Payokakamu'i is known for bringing harm to people.<ref name="akuma">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Atsushi Yamakita|ja|山北篤}}, supervised by Sato Toshiyuki | title=悪魔事典 | publisher=[[Shinkigensha]] | series=Truth In Fantasy | year=2000 | pages=232-233 | isbn=978-4-88317-353-2}}</ref> The deity governs {{ill|smallpox|ja|天然痘}} and other contagious diseases. One day, while telling stories to his children, the father realized Payokakamu'i was outside their home. Offering a sacrifice, the deity appeared in his dreams, expressing gratitude for the offering and the stories, and revealed a method to avoid illness before leaving. When the father shared this method with the village chief, the village expressed gratitude, and the villagers were spared from the epidemic, living happily ever after.<ref name="akuma" /> Inspired by this story, rituals were performed to pray to Payokakamu'i for the end of epidemics during outbreaks.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20200418125506/https://this.kiji.is/624148580355900513 "Payokakamu'i," Stay Away: Ainu Perform Prayer Rituals] - {{ill|Kyodo News|ja|共同通信}}</ref>
A mysterious figure called '''Kasunte''' was said to have been born between Payokakamu'i and a human. Kasunte repeatedly reincarnated after being killed, but the Ainu of {{ill|Akkeshi District|ja|厚岸郡}} finally ended this cycle by separating its upper and lower jaws. However, many of these Ainu succumbed to smallpox, and those who survived were later killed by a {{ill|tsunami|ja|津波}}. This led to the belief that the tsunami was Kasunte's curse.<ref>妖怪事典, p. 105.</ref>

'''Payokakamu'i''' (パヨカカムイ) or '''Paikaikamu'i''' (パイカイカムイ) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} of {{ill|epidemics|ja|疫病}} in {{ill|Ainu mythology|ja|アイヌ}}.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Kenji Murakami|ja|村上健司}} | title=妖怪事典 | publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] | year=2000 | pages=267-268 | isbn=978-4-6203-1428-0}}</ref>
Unlike most Ainu deities, Payokakamu'i is known for bringing harm to people.<ref name="akuma">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Atsushi Yamakita|ja|山北篤}}, supervised by Sato Toshiyuki | title=悪魔事典 | publisher=[[Shinkigensha]] | series=Truth In Fantasy | year=2000 | pages=232-233 | isbn=978-4-88317-353-2}}</ref> The deity governs {{ill|smallpox|ja|天然痘}} and other contagious diseases. It is said to shoot invisible arrows, and those who hear the sound of these arrows are afflicted with smallpox.<ref name="murakami" />
According to one tale, a poor Ainu family lived on the outskirts of a village. The father was a poor hunter but a talented storyteller of {{ill|yukar|ja|ユーカラ}}, traditional narrative songs. One day, while telling stories to his children, the father realized Payokakamu'i was outside their home. Offering a sacrifice, the deity appeared in his dreams, expressing gratitude for the offering and the stories, and revealed a method to avoid illness before leaving. When the father shared this method with the village chief, the village expressed gratitude, and the villagers were spared from the epidemic, living happily ever after.<ref name="akuma" /> Inspired by this story, rituals were performed to pray to Payokakamu'i for the end of epidemics during outbreaks.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20200418125506/https://this.kiji.is/624148580355900513 "Payokakamu'i," Stay Away: Ainu Perform Prayer Rituals] - {{ill|Kyodo News|ja|共同通信}}</ref>
A mysterious figure called '''Kasunte''' was said to have been born between Payokakamu'i and a human. Kasunte repeatedly reincarnated after being killed, but the Ainu of {{ill|Akkeshi District|ja|厚岸郡}} finally ended this cycle by separating its upper and lower jaws. However, many of these Ainu succumbed to smallpox, and those who survived were later killed by a {{ill|tsunami|ja|津波}}. This led to the belief that the tsunami was Kasunte's curse.<ref>妖怪事典, p. 105.</ref>

'''Payokakamu'i''' (パヨカカムイ) or '''Paikaikamu'i''' (パイカイカムイ) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} of {{ill|epidemics|ja|疫病}} in {{ill|Ainu mythology|ja|アイヌ}}.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Kenji Murakami|ja|村上健司}} | title=妖怪事典 | publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] | year=2000 | pages=267-268 | isbn=978-4-6203-1428-0}}</ref>
Unlike most Ainu deities, Payokakamu'i is known for bringing harm to people.<ref name="akuma">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Atsushi Yamakita|ja|山北篤}}, supervised by Sato Toshiyuki | title=悪魔事典 | publisher=[[Shinkigensha]] | series=Truth In Fantasy | year=2000 | pages=232-233 | isbn=978-4-88317-353-2}}</ref> The deity governs {{ill|smallpox|ja|天然痘}} and other contagious diseases. It is said to shoot invisible arrows, and those who hear the sound of these arrows are afflicted with smallpox.<ref name="murakami" />
According to one tale, a poor Ainu family lived on the outskirts of a village. The father was a poor hunter but a talented storyteller of {{ill|yukar|ja|ユーカラ}}, traditional narrative songs. One day, while telling stories to his children, the father realized Payokakamu'i was outside their home. Offering a sacrifice, the deity appeared in his dreams, expressing gratitude for the offering and the stories, and revealed a method to avoid illness before leaving. When the father shared this method with the village chief, the village expressed gratitude, and the villagers were spared from the epidemic, living happily ever after.<ref name="akuma" /> Inspired by this story, rituals were performed to pray to Payokakamu'i for the end of epidemics during outbreaks.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20200418125506/https://this.kiji.is/624148580355900513 "Payokakamu'i," Stay Away: Ainu Perform Prayer Rituals] - {{ill|Kyodo News|ja|共同通信}}</ref>
A mysterious figure called '''Kasunte''' was said to have been born between Payokakamu'i and a human. Kasunte repeatedly reincarnated after being killed, but the Ainu of {{ill|Akkeshi District|ja|厚岸郡}} finally ended this cycle by separating its upper and lower jaws. However, many of these Ainu succumbed to smallpox, and those who survived were later killed by a {{ill|tsunami|ja|津波}}. This led to the belief that the tsunami was Kasunte's curse.<ref>妖怪事典, p. 105.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * {{ill|Mintuci|ja|ミントゥチ}}

A mysterious figure called '''Kasunte''' was said to have been born between Payokakamu'i and a human. Kasunte repeatedly reincarnated after being killed, but the Ainu of {{ill|Akkeshi District|ja|厚岸郡}} finally ended this cycle by separating its upper and lower jaws. However, many of these Ainu succumbed to smallpox, and those who survived were later killed by a {{ill|tsunami|ja|津波}}. This led to the belief that the tsunami was Kasunte's curse.<ref>妖怪事典, p. 105.</ref>

'''Payokakamu'i''' (パヨカカムイ) or '''Paikaikamu'i''' (パイカイカムイ) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} of {{ill|epidemics|ja|疫病}} in {{ill|Ainu mythology|ja|アイヌ}}.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Kenji Murakami|ja|村上健司}} | title=妖怪事典 | publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] | year=2000 | pages=267-268 | isbn=978-4-6203-1428-0}}</ref>
Unlike most Ainu deities, Payokakamu'i is known for bringing harm to people.<ref name="akuma">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Atsushi Yamakita|ja|山北篤}}, supervised by Sato Toshiyuki | title=悪魔事典 | publisher=[[Shinkigensha]] | series=Truth In Fantasy | year=2000 | pages=232-233 | isbn=978-4-88317-353-2}}</ref> The deity governs {{ill|smallpox|ja|天然痘}} and other contagious diseases. It is said to shoot invisible arrows, and those who hear the sound of these arrows are afflicted with smallpox.<ref name="murakami" />
According to one tale, a poor Ainu family lived on the outskirts of a village. The father was a poor hunter but a talented storyteller of {{ill|yukar|ja|ユーカラ}}, traditional narrative songs. One day, while telling stories to his children, the father realized Payokakamu'i was outside their home. Offering a sacrifice, the deity appeared in his dreams, expressing gratitude for the offering and the stories, and revealed a method to avoid illness before leaving. When the father shared this method with the village chief, the village expressed gratitude, and the villagers were spared from the epidemic, living happily ever after.<ref name="akuma" /> Inspired by this story, rituals were performed to pray to Payokakamu'i for the end of epidemics during outbreaks.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20200418125506/https://this.kiji.is/624148580355900513 "Payokakamu'i," Stay Away: Ainu Perform Prayer Rituals] - {{ill|Kyodo News|ja|共同通信}}</ref>
A mysterious figure called '''Kasunte''' was said to have been born between Payokakamu'i and a human. Kasunte repeatedly reincarnated after being killed, but the Ainu of {{ill|Akkeshi District|ja|厚岸郡}} finally ended this cycle by separating its upper and lower jaws. However, many of these Ainu succumbed to smallpox, and those who survived were later killed by a {{ill|tsunami|ja|津波}}. This led to the belief that the tsunami was Kasunte's curse.<ref>妖怪事典, p. 105.</ref>

A mysterious figure called '''Kasunte''' was said to have been born between Payokakamu'i and a human. Kasunte repeatedly reincarnated after being killed, but the Ainu of {{ill|Akkeshi District|ja|厚岸郡}} finally ended this cycle by separating its upper and lower jaws. However, many of these Ainu succumbed to smallpox, and those who survived were later killed by a {{ill|tsunami|ja|津波}}. This led to the belief that the tsunami was Kasunte's curse.<ref>妖怪事典, p. 105.</ref>

revision.text:
'''Payokakamu'i''' (パヨカカムイ) or '''Paikaikamu'i''' (パイカイカムイ) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} of {{ill|epidemics|ja|疫病}} in {{ill|Ainu mythology|ja|アイヌ}}.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Kenji Murakami|ja|村上健司}} | title=妖怪事典 | publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] | year=2000 | pages=267-268 | isbn=978-4-6203-1428-0}}</ref>
Unlike most Ainu deities, Payokakamu'i is known for bringing harm to people.<ref name="akuma">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Atsushi Yamakita|ja|山北篤}}, supervised by Sato Toshiyuki | title=悪魔事典 | publisher=[[Shinkigensha]] | series=Truth In Fantasy | year=2000 | pages=232-233 | isbn=978-4-88317-353-2}}</ref> The deity governs {{ill|smallpox|ja|天然痘}} and other contagious diseases. It is said to shoot invisible arrows, and those who hear the sound of these arrows are afflicted with smallpox.<ref name="murakami" />
According to one tale, a poor Ainu family lived on the outskirts of a village. The father was a poor hunter but a talented storyteller of {{ill|yukar|ja|ユーカラ}}, traditional narrative songs. One day, while telling stories to his children, the father realized Payokakamu'i was outside their home. Offering a sacrifice, the deity appeared in his dreams, expressing gratitude for the offering and the stories, and revealed a method to avoid illness before leaving. When the father shared this method with the village chief, the village expressed gratitude, and the villagers were spared from the epidemic, living happily ever after.<ref name="akuma" /> Inspired by this story, rituals were performed to pray to Payokakamu'i for the end of epidemics during outbreaks.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20200418125506/https://this.kiji.is/624148580355900513 "Payokakamu'i," Stay Away: Ainu Perform Prayer Rituals] - {{ill|Kyodo News|ja|共同通信}}</ref>
A mysterious figure called '''Kasunte''' was said to have been born between Payokakamu'i and a human. Kasunte repeatedly reincarnated after being killed, but the Ainu of {{ill|Akkeshi District|ja|厚岸郡}} finally ended this cycle by separating its upper and lower jaws. However, many of these Ainu succumbed to smallpox, and those who survived were later killed by a {{ill|tsunami|ja|津波}}. This led to the belief that the tsunami was Kasunte's curse.<ref>妖怪事典, p. 105.</ref>

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Completion tier 1]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Payokakamu'i''' (パヨカカムイ) or '''Paikaikamu'i''' (パイカイカムイ) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} of {{ill|epidemics|ja|疫病}} in {{ill|Ainu mythology|ja|アイヌ}}.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Kenji Murakami|ja|村上健司}} | title=妖怪事典 | publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] | year=2000 | pages=267-268 | isbn=978-4-6203-1428-0}}</ref>
Unlike most Ainu deities, Payokakamu'i is known for bringing harm to people.<ref name="akuma">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Atsushi Yamakita|ja|山北篤}}, supervised by Sato Toshiyuki | title=悪魔事典 | publisher=[[Shinkigensha]] | series=Truth In Fantasy | year=2000 | pages=232-233 | isbn=978-4-88317-353-2}}</ref> The deity governs {{ill|smallpox|ja|天然痘}} and other contagious diseases. It is said to shoot invisible arrows, and those who hear the sound of these arrows are afflicted with smallpox.<ref name="murakami" />
According to one tale, a poor Ainu family lived on the outskirts of a village. The father was a poor hunter but a talented storyteller of {{ill|yukar|ja|ユーカラ}}, traditional narrative songs. One day, while telling stories to his children, the father realized Payokakamu'i was outside their home. Offering a sacrifice, the deity appeared in his dreams, expressing gratitude for the offering and the stories, and revealed a method to avoid illness before leaving. When the father shared this method with the village chief, the village expressed gratitude, and the villagers were spared from the epidemic, living happily ever after.<ref name="akuma" /> Inspired by this story, rituals were performed to pray to Payokakamu'i for the end of epidemics during outbreaks.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20200418125506/https://this.kiji.is/624148580355900513 "Payokakamu'i," Stay Away: Ainu Perform Prayer Rituals] - {{ill|Kyodo News|ja|共同通信}}</ref>
A mysterious figure called '''Kasunte''' was said to have been born between Payokakamu'i and a human. Kasunte repeatedly reincarnated after being killed, but the Ainu of {{ill|Akkeshi District|ja|厚岸郡}} finally ended this cycle by separating its upper and lower jaws. However, many of these Ainu succumbed to smallpox, and those who survived were later killed by a {{ill|tsunami|ja|津波}}. This led to the belief that the tsunami was Kasunte's curse.<ref>妖怪事典, p. 105.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * {{ill|Mintuci|ja|ミントゥチ}}

'''Payokakamu'i''' (パヨカカムイ) or '''Paikaikamu'i''' (パイカイカムイ) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} of {{ill|epidemics|ja|疫病}} in {{ill|Ainu mythology|ja|アイヌ}}.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Kenji Murakami|ja|村上健司}} | title=妖怪事典 | publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] | year=2000 | pages=267-268 | isbn=978-4-6203-1428-0}}</ref>
Unlike most Ainu deities, Payokakamu'i is known for bringing harm to people.<ref name="akuma">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Atsushi Yamakita|ja|山北篤}}, supervised by Sato Toshiyuki | title=悪魔事典 | publisher=[[Shinkigensha]] | series=Truth In Fantasy | year=2000 | pages=232-233 | isbn=978-4-88317-353-2}}</ref> The deity governs {{ill|smallpox|ja|天然痘}} and other contagious diseases. It is said to shoot invisible arrows, and those who hear the sound of these arrows are afflicted with smallpox.<ref name="murakami" />
According to one tale, a poor Ainu family lived on the outskirts of a village. The father was a poor hunter but a talented storyteller of {{ill|yukar|ja|ユーカラ}}, traditional narrative songs. One day, while telling stories to his children, the father realized Payokakamu'i was outside their home. Offering a sacrifice, the deity appeared in his dreams, expressing gratitude for the offering and the stories, and revealed a method to avoid illness before leaving. When the father shared this method with the village chief, the village expressed gratitude, and the villagers were spared from the epidemic, living happily ever after.<ref name="akuma" /> Inspired by this story, rituals were performed to pray to Payokakamu'i for the end of epidemics during outbreaks.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20200418125506/https://this.kiji.is/624148580355900513 "Payokakamu'i," Stay Away: Ainu Perform Prayer Rituals] - {{ill|Kyodo News|ja|共同通信}}</ref>
A mysterious figure called '''Kasunte''' was said to have been born between Payokakamu'i and a human. Kasunte repeatedly reincarnated after being killed, but the Ainu of {{ill|Akkeshi District|ja|厚岸郡}} finally ended this cycle by separating its upper and lower jaws. However, many of these Ainu succumbed to smallpox, and those who survived were later killed by a {{ill|tsunami|ja|津波}}. This led to the belief that the tsunami was Kasunte's curse.<ref>妖怪事典, p. 105.</ref>

'''Payokakamu'i''' (パヨカカムイ) or '''Paikaikamu'i''' (パイカイカムイ) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} of {{ill|epidemics|ja|疫病}} in {{ill|Ainu mythology|ja|アイヌ}}.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Kenji Murakami|ja|村上健司}} | title=妖怪事典 | publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] | year=2000 | pages=267-268 | isbn=978-4-6203-1428-0}}</ref>
Unlike most Ainu deities, Payokakamu'i is known for bringing harm to people.<ref name="akuma">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Atsushi Yamakita|ja|山北篤}}, supervised by Sato Toshiyuki | title=悪魔事典 | publisher=[[Shinkigensha]] | series=Truth In Fantasy | year=2000 | pages=232-233 | isbn=978-4-88317-353-2}}</ref> The deity governs {{ill|smallpox|ja|天然痘}} and other contagious diseases. It is said to shoot invisible arrows, and those who hear the sound of these arrows are afflicted with smallpox.<ref name="murakami" />
According to one tale, a poor Ainu family lived on the outskirts of a village. The father was a poor hunter but a talented storyteller of {{ill|yukar|ja|ユーカラ}}, traditional narrative songs. One day, while telling stories to his children, the father realized Payokakamu'i was outside their home. Offering a sacrifice, the deity appeared in his dreams, expressing gratitude for the offering and the stories, and revealed a method to avoid illness before leaving. When the father shared this method with the village chief, the village expressed gratitude, and the villagers were spared from the epidemic, living happily ever after.<ref name="akuma" /> Inspired by this story, rituals were performed to pray to Payokakamu'i for the end of epidemics during outbreaks.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20200418125506/https://this.kiji.is/624148580355900513 "Payokakamu'i," Stay Away: Ainu Perform Prayer Rituals] - {{ill|Kyodo News|ja|共同通信}}</ref>
A mysterious figure called '''Kasunte''' was said to have been born between Payokakamu'i and a human. Kasunte repeatedly reincarnated after being killed, but the Ainu of {{ill|Akkeshi District|ja|厚岸郡}} finally ended this cycle by separating its upper and lower jaws. However, many of these Ainu succumbed to smallpox, and those who survived were later killed by a {{ill|tsunami|ja|津波}}. This led to the belief that the tsunami was Kasunte's curse.<ref>妖怪事典, p. 105.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * {{ill|Mintuci|ja|ミントゥチ}}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Completion tier 1]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Payokakamu'i''' (パヨカカムイ) or '''Paikaikamu'i''' (パイカイカムイ) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} of {{ill|epidemics|ja|疫病}} in {{ill|Ainu mythology|ja|アイヌ}}.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Kenji Murakami|ja|村上健司}} | title=妖怪事典 | publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] | year=2000 | pages=267-268 | isbn=978-4-6203-1428-0}}</ref>
Unlike most Ainu deities, Payokakamu'i is known for bringing harm to people.<ref name="akuma">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Atsushi Yamakita|ja|山北篤}}, supervised by Sato Toshiyuki | title=悪魔事典 | publisher=[[Shinkigensha]] | series=Truth In Fantasy | year=2000 | pages=232-233 | isbn=978-4-88317-353-2}}</ref> The deity governs {{ill|smallpox|ja|天然痘}} and other contagious diseases. It is said to shoot invisible arrows, and those who hear the sound of these arrows are afflicted with smallpox.<ref name="murakami" />
According to one tale, a poor Ainu family lived on the outskirts of a village. The father was a poor hunter but a talented storyteller of {{ill|yukar|ja|ユーカラ}}, traditional narrative songs. One day, while telling stories to his children, the father realized Payokakamu'i was outside their home. Offering a sacrifice, the deity appeared in his dreams, expressing gratitude for the offering and the stories, and revealed a method to avoid illness before leaving. When the father shared this method with the village chief, the village expressed gratitude, and the villagers were spared from the epidemic, living happily ever after.<ref name="akuma" /> Inspired by this story, rituals were performed to pray to Payokakamu'i for the end of epidemics during outbreaks.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20200418125506/https://this.kiji.is/624148580355900513 "Payokakamu'i," Stay Away: Ainu Perform Prayer Rituals] - {{ill|Kyodo News|ja|共同通信}}</ref>
A mysterious figure called '''Kasunte''' was said to have been born between Payokakamu'i and a human. Kasunte repeatedly reincarnated after being killed, but the Ainu of {{ill|Akkeshi District|ja|厚岸郡}} finally ended this cycle by separating its upper and lower jaws. However, many of these Ainu succumbed to smallpox, and those who survived were later killed by a {{ill|tsunami|ja|津波}}. This led to the belief that the tsunami was Kasunte's curse.<ref>妖怪事典, p. 105.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * {{ill|Mintuci|ja|ミントゥチ}}

'''Payokakamu'i''' (パヨカカムイ) or '''Paikaikamu'i''' (パイカイカムイ) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神}} of {{ill|epidemics|ja|疫病}} in {{ill|Ainu mythology|ja|アイヌ}}.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Kenji Murakami|ja|村上健司}} | title=妖怪事典 | publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] | year=2000 | pages=267-268 | isbn=978-4-6203-1428-0}}</ref>
Unlike most Ainu deities, Payokakamu'i is known for bringing harm to people.<ref name="akuma">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Atsushi Yamakita|ja|山北篤}}, supervised by Sato Toshiyuki | title=悪魔事典 | publisher=[[Shinkigensha]] | series=Truth In Fantasy | year=2000 | pages=232-233 | isbn=978-4-88317-353-2}}</ref> The deity governs {{ill|smallpox|ja|天然痘}} and other contagious diseases. It is said to shoot invisible arrows, and those who hear the sound of these arrows are afflicted with smallpox.<ref name="murakami" />
According to one tale, a poor Ainu family lived on the outskirts of a village. The father was a poor hunter but a talented storyteller of {{ill|yukar|ja|ユーカラ}}, traditional narrative songs. One day, while telling stories to his children, the father realized Payokakamu'i was outside their home. Offering a sacrifice, the deity appeared in his dreams, expressing gratitude for the offering and the stories, and revealed a method to avoid illness before leaving. When the father shared this method with the village chief, the village expressed gratitude, and the villagers were spared from the epidemic, living happily ever after.<ref name="akuma" /> Inspired by this story, rituals were performed to pray to Payokakamu'i for the end of epidemics during outbreaks.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20200418125506/https://this.kiji.is/624148580355900513 "Payokakamu'i," Stay Away: Ainu Perform Prayer Rituals] - {{ill|Kyodo News|ja|共同通信}}</ref>
A mysterious figure called '''Kasunte''' was said to have been born between Payokakamu'i and a human. Kasunte repeatedly reincarnated after being killed, but the Ainu of {{ill|Akkeshi District|ja|厚岸郡}} finally ended this cycle by separating its upper and lower jaws. However, many of these Ainu succumbed to smallpox, and those who survived were later killed by a {{ill|tsunami|ja|津波}}. This led to the belief that the tsunami was Kasunte's curse.<ref>妖怪事典, p. 105.</ref>

The father was a poor hunter but a talented storyteller of {{ill|yukar|ja|ユーカラ|12=draft|12=draft}}, traditional narrative songs. One day, while telling stories to his children, the father realized Payokakamu'i was outside their home. Offering a sacrifice, the deity appeared in his dreams, expressing gratitude for the offering and the stories, and revealed a method to avoid illness before leaving. When the father shared this method with the village chief, the village expressed gratitude, and the villagers were spared from the epidemic, living happily ever after.<ref name="akuma" /> Inspired by this story, rituals were performed to pray to Payokakamu'i for the end of epidemics during outbreaks.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20200418125506/https://this.kiji.is/624148580355900513 "Payokakamu'i," Stay Away: Ainu Perform Prayer Rituals] - {{ill|Kyodo News|ja|共同通信|12=draft|12=draft}}</ref>
A mysterious figure called '''Kasunte''' was said to have been born between Payokakamu'i and a human. Kasunte repeatedly reincarnated after being killed, but the Ainu of {{ill|Akkeshi District|ja|厚岸郡|12=draft|12=draft}} finally ended this cycle by separating its upper and lower jaws. However, many of these Ainu succumbed to smallpox, and those who survived were later killed by a {{ill|tsunami|ja|津波|12=draft|12=draft}}. This led to the belief that the tsunami was Kasunte's curse.<ref>妖怪事典, p. 105.</ref>

It is said to shoot invisible arrows, and those who hear the sound of these arrows are afflicted with smallpox.<ref name="murakami" />

The father was a poor hunter but a talented storyteller of {{ill|yukar|ja|ユーカラ|12=draft|12=draft}}, traditional narrative songs. When the father shared this method with the village chief, the village expressed gratitude, and the villagers were spared from the epidemic, living happily ever after.<ref name="akuma" /> Inspired by this story, rituals were performed to pray to Payokakamu'i for the end of epidemics during outbreaks.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20200418125506/https://this.kiji.is/624148580355900513 "Payokakamu'i," Stay Away: Ainu Perform Prayer Rituals] - {{ill|Kyodo News|ja|共同通信|12=draft|12=draft}}</ref>

However, many of these Ainu succumbed to smallpox, and those who survived were later killed by a {{ill|tsunami|ja|津波|12=draft|12=draft}}. This led to the belief that the tsunami was Kasunte's curse.<ref>妖怪事典, p. 105.</ref>

revision.text:
'''パヨカカムイ'''または'''パイカイカムイ'''は、[[アイヌ]]に伝わる[[疫病]]の[[神]]<ref name="murakami">[[村上健司]]編著 『妖怪事典』 [[毎日新聞社]]、2000年、267-268頁。ISBN 978-4-6203-1428-0。</ref>。
アイヌの神にしては珍しく、人々に害をもたらすのが役目とされる<ref name="akuma">[[山北篤]]・佐藤俊之監修 『悪魔事典』 [[新紀元社]]〈Truth In Fantasy〉、2000年、232-233頁。ISBN 978-4-88317-353-2。</ref>。[[天然痘|疱瘡（天然痘）]]や流行病を司る神であり、姿を見せずに弓を放ち、この矢を射る音を聞いた者は疱瘡に侵されるといわれる<ref name="murakami" />。
とある村のはずれに、貧しいアイヌの一家が住んでいた。そこの父親は狩りが下手であったが、[[ユーカラ]]（民話詩）を語るのは上手だった。ある時、その父親が子供達にユーカラを語っていると、家の外にパヨカカムイがいることに気づいた。そこで捧げ物をすると、パヨカカムイは夢枕に現れ、上手なユーカラと捧げ物の礼をいい、病を避ける方法を伝えて去っていった。この方法を村長に伝えたところ、村長は感謝し、村の者は皆疫病に冒されること無く、幸せに暮らしたとされる<ref name="akuma" />。この言い伝えを元に疫病が流行した際には、パヨカカムイに疫病を終息を願う儀式が行われるようになった<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20200418125506/https://this.kiji.is/624148580355900513 「パヨカカムイ」、近づかないで アイヌ民族が祈りの儀式] - [[共同通信]]</ref>。
また、このパヨカカムイと人間との間には'''カスンテ'''という怪人物が生まれた。カスンテは何度殺されても生まれ変わったが、[[厚岸郡|厚岸]]のアイヌたちが上下の顎を別々にすることで生まれ変わることはなくなった。しかし、そのアイヌたちの多くは疱瘡を煩って死に、わずかに残った者たちも[[津波]]に遭って死んだ。そのために津波はカスンテの祟りといわれたという<ref>『妖怪事典』 105頁。</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 関連項目== * [[ミントゥチ]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:はよかかむい}} [[Category:アイヌの伝承]] [[Category:アイヌの神]] [[Category:天然痘]]

revision.text:
'''Payokakamu'i''' (パヨカカムイ) or '''Paikaikamu'i''' (パイカイカムイ) is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]] of {{ill|epidemics|ja|疫病|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/epidemics|qq=}} in [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu mythology]].<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Kenji Murakami|ja|村上健司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenji Murakami|qq=}} | title=妖怪事典 | publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] | year=2000 | pages=267-268 | isbn=978-4-6203-1428-0}}</ref>
Unlike most Ainu deities, Payokakamu'i is known for bringing harm to people.<ref name="akuma">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Atsushi Yamakita|ja|山北篤|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atsushi Yamakita|qq=}}, supervised by Sato Toshiyuki | title=悪魔事典 | publisher=[[Shinkigensha]] | series=Truth In Fantasy | year=2000 | pages=232-233 | isbn=978-4-88317-353-2}}</ref> The deity governs [[:en:Smallpox|smallpox]] and other contagious diseases. It is said to shoot invisible arrows, and those who hear the sound of these arrows are afflicted with smallpox.<ref name="murakami" />
According to one tale, a poor Ainu family lived on the outskirts of a village. The father was a poor hunter but a talented storyteller of [[:en:Yukar|yukar]], traditional narrative songs. One day, while telling stories to his children, the father realized Payokakamu'i was outside their home. Offering a sacrifice, the deity appeared in his dreams, expressing gratitude for the offering and the stories, and revealed a method to avoid illness before leaving. When the father shared this method with the village chief, the village expressed gratitude, and the villagers were spared from the epidemic, living happily ever after.<ref name="akuma" /> Inspired by this story, rituals were performed to pray to Payokakamu'i for the end of epidemics during outbreaks.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20200418125506/https://this.kiji.is/624148580355900513 "Payokakamu'i," Stay Away: Ainu Perform Prayer Rituals] - {{ill|Kyodo News|ja|共同通信|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyodo News|qq=}}</ref>
A mysterious figure called '''Kasunte''' was said to have been born between Payokakamu'i and a human. Kasunte repeatedly reincarnated after being killed, but the Ainu of [[:en:Akkeshi District, Hokkaido|Akkeshi District]] finally ended this cycle by separating its upper and lower jaws. However, many of these Ainu succumbed to smallpox, and those who survived were later killed by a [[:en:Tsunami|tsunami]]. This led to the belief that the tsunami was Kasunte's curse.<ref>妖怪事典, p. 105.</ref>

'''Payokakamu'i''' (パヨカカムイ) or '''Paikaikamu'i''' (パイカイカムイ) is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]] of {{ill|epidemics|ja|疫病|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/epidemics|qq=}} in [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu mythology]].<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Kenji Murakami|ja|村上健司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenji Murakami|qq=}} | title=妖怪事典 | publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] | year=2000 | pages=267-268 | isbn=978-4-6203-1428-0}}</ref>
Unlike most Ainu deities, Payokakamu'i is known for bringing harm to people.<ref name="akuma">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Atsushi Yamakita|ja|山北篤|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atsushi Yamakita|qq=}}, supervised by Sato Toshiyuki | title=悪魔事典 | publisher=[[Shinkigensha]] | series=Truth In Fantasy | year=2000 | pages=232-233 | isbn=978-4-88317-353-2}}</ref> The deity governs [[:en:Smallpox|smallpox]] and other contagious diseases. It is said to shoot invisible arrows, and those who hear the sound of these arrows are afflicted with smallpox.<ref name="murakami" />
According to one tale, a poor Ainu family lived on the outskirts of a village. The father was a poor hunter but a talented storyteller of [[:en:Yukar|yukar]], traditional narrative songs. One day, while telling stories to his children, the father realized Payokakamu'i was outside their home. Offering a sacrifice, the deity appeared in his dreams, expressing gratitude for the offering and the stories, and revealed a method to avoid illness before leaving. When the father shared this method with the village chief, the village expressed gratitude, and the villagers were spared from the epidemic, living happily ever after.<ref name="akuma" /> Inspired by this story, rituals were performed to pray to Payokakamu'i for the end of epidemics during outbreaks.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20200418125506/https://this.kiji.is/624148580355900513 "Payokakamu'i," Stay Away: Ainu Perform Prayer Rituals] - {{ill|Kyodo News|ja|共同通信|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyodo News|qq=}}</ref>
A mysterious figure called '''Kasunte''' was said to have been born between Payokakamu'i and a human. Kasunte repeatedly reincarnated after being killed, but the Ainu of [[:en:Akkeshi District, Hokkaido|Akkeshi District]] finally ended this cycle by separating its upper and lower jaws. However, many of these Ainu succumbed to smallpox, and those who survived were later killed by a [[:en:Tsunami|tsunami]]. This led to the belief that the tsunami was Kasunte's curse.<ref>妖怪事典, p. 105.</ref>

When the father shared this method with the village chief, the village expressed gratitude, and the villagers were spared from the epidemic, living happily ever after.<ref name="akuma" /> Inspired by this story, rituals were performed to pray to Payokakamu'i for the end of epidemics during outbreaks.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20200418125506/https://this.kiji.is/624148580355900513 "Payokakamu'i," Stay Away: Ainu Perform Prayer Rituals] - {{ill|Kyodo News|ja|共同通信|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyodo News|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 共同通信社|1=Kyodo News|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q779730}}</ref>
A mysterious figure called '''Kasunte''' was said to have been born between Payokakamu'i and a human. Kasunte repeatedly reincarnated after being killed, but the Ainu of [[:en:Akkeshi District, Hokkaido|Akkeshi District]] finally ended this cycle by separating its upper and lower jaws. However, many of these Ainu succumbed to smallpox, and those who survived were later killed by a [[:en:Tsunami|tsunami]]. This led to the belief that the tsunami was Kasunte's curse.<ref>妖怪事典, p. 105.</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:パヨカカムイ]][[ko:파요카카무이]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11280189]] Wikidata name: Payoka Kamuy [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Payokakamu'i''' (パヨカカムイ) or '''Paikaikamu'i''' (パイカイカムイ) is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]] of {{ill|epidemics|ja|疫病|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/epidemics|1=ekibyou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17239211}} in [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu mythology]].<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Kenji Murakami|ja|村上健司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenji Murakami|1=Kenji Murakami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11522709}} | title=妖怪事典 | publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] | year=2000 | pages=267-268 | isbn=978-4-6203-1428-0}}</ref>
Unlike most Ainu deities, Payokakamu'i is known for bringing harm to people.<ref name="akuma">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Atsushi Yamakita|ja|山北篤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atsushi Yamakita|1=Atsushi Yamakita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11466547}}, supervised by Sato Toshiyuki | title=悪魔事典 | publisher=[[Shinkigensha]] | series=Truth In Fantasy | year=2000 | pages=232-233 | isbn=978-4-88317-353-2}}</ref> The deity governs [[:en:Smallpox|smallpox]] and other contagious diseases. It is said to shoot invisible arrows, and those who hear the sound of these arrows are afflicted with smallpox.<ref name="murakami" />
According to one tale, a poor Ainu family lived on the outskirts of a village. The father was a poor hunter but a talented storyteller of [[:en:Yukar|yukar]], traditional narrative songs. One day, while telling stories to his children, the father realized Payokakamu'i was outside their home. Offering a sacrifice, the deity appeared in his dreams, expressing gratitude for the offering and the stories, and revealed a method to avoid illness before leaving. When the father shared this method with the village chief, the village expressed gratitude, and the villagers were spared from the epidemic, living happily ever after.<ref name="akuma" /> Inspired by this story, rituals were performed to pray to Payokakamu'i for the end of epidemics during outbreaks.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20200418125506/https://this.kiji.is/624148580355900513 "Payokakamu'i," Stay Away: Ainu Perform Prayer Rituals] - {{ill|Kyodo News|ja|共同通信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyodo News|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 共同通信社|1=Kyodo News|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q779730}}</ref>
A mysterious figure called '''Kasunte''' was said to have been born between Payokakamu'i and a human. Kasunte repeatedly reincarnated after being killed, but the Ainu of [[:en:Akkeshi District, Hokkaido|Akkeshi District]] finally ended this cycle by separating its upper and lower jaws. However, many of these Ainu succumbed to smallpox, and those who survived were later killed by a [[:en:Tsunami|tsunami]]. This led to the belief that the tsunami was Kasunte's curse.<ref>妖怪事典, p. 105.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[:en:Mintuci|Mintuci]]
{{translated page|ja|パヨカカムイ|version=98493717|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:パヨカカムイ]][[ko:파요카카무이]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11280189]] Wikidata name: Payoka Kamuy [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Payokakamu'i''' (パヨカカムイ) or '''Paikaikamu'i''' (パイカイカムイ) is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]] of {{ill|epidemics|ja|疫病|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/epidemics|1=ekibyou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17239211}} in [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu mythology]].<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Kenji Murakami|ja|村上健司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenji Murakami|1=Kenji Murakami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11522709}} | title=妖怪事典 | publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] | year=2000 | pages=267-268 | isbn=978-4-6203-1428-0}}</ref>

It is said to shoot invisible arrows, and those who hear the sound of these arrows are afflicted with smallpox.<ref name="murakami" />
According to one tale, a poor Ainu family lived on the outskirts of a village. The father was a poor hunter but a talented storyteller of [[:en:Yukar|yukar]], traditional narrative songs. One day, while telling stories to his children, the father realized Payokakamu'i was outside their home. Offering a sacrifice, the deity appeared in his dreams, expressing gratitude for the offering and the stories, and revealed a method to avoid illness before leaving. When the father shared this method with the village chief, the village expressed gratitude, and the villagers were spared from the epidemic, living happily ever after.<ref name="akuma" /> Inspired by this story, rituals were performed to pray to Payokakamu'i for the end of epidemics during outbreaks.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20200418125506/https://this.kiji.is/624148580355900513 "Payokakamu'i," Stay Away: Ainu Perform Prayer Rituals] - {{ill|Kyodo News|ja|共同通信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyodo News|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 共同通信社|1=Kyodo News|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q779730}}</ref>
A mysterious figure called '''Kasunte''' was said to have been born between Payokakamu'i and a human. Kasunte repeatedly reincarnated after being killed, but the Ainu of [[:en:Akkeshi District, Hokkaido|Akkeshi District]] finally ended this cycle by separating its upper and lower jaws. However, many of these Ainu succumbed to smallpox, and those who survived were later killed by a [[:en:Tsunami|tsunami]]. This led to the belief that the tsunami was Kasunte's curse.<ref>妖怪事典, p. 105.</ref>

However, many of these Ainu succumbed to smallpox, and those who survived were later killed by a [[:en:Tsunami|tsunami]]. This led to the belief that the tsunami was Kasunte's curse.<ref>妖怪事典, p. 105.</ref>

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:パヨカカムイ]][[ko:파요카카무이]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:パヨカカムイ]][[ko:파요카카무이]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:パヨカカムイ]][[ko:파요카카무이]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11280189]] Wikidata name: Payoka Kamuy [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:53, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Payokakamu'i''' (パヨカカムイ) or '''Paikaikamu'i''' (パイカイカムイ) is a {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=Deity}} of {{ill|epidemics|ja|疫病|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/epidemics|1=ekibyou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17239211}} in {{ill|Ainu people|en|Ainu people|WD=Q101828|lt=Ainu mythology}}.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Kenji Murakami|ja|村上健司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenji Murakami|1=Kenji Murakami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11522709}} | title=妖怪事典 | publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] | year=2000 | pages=267-268 | isbn=978-4-6203-1428-0}}</ref>
Unlike most Ainu deities, Payokakamu'i is known for bringing harm to people.<ref name="akuma">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Atsushi Yamakita|ja|山北篤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atsushi Yamakita|1=Atsushi Yamakita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11466547}}, supervised by Sato Toshiyuki | title=悪魔事典 | publisher=[[Shinkigensha]] | series=Truth In Fantasy | year=2000 | pages=232-233 | isbn=978-4-88317-353-2}}</ref> The deity governs {{ill|Smallpox|en|Smallpox|WD=Q12214|lt=smallpox}} and other contagious diseases. It is said to shoot invisible arrows, and those who hear the sound of these arrows are afflicted with smallpox.<ref name="murakami" />
According to one tale, a poor Ainu family lived on the outskirts of a village. The father was a poor hunter but a talented storyteller of {{ill|Yukar|en|Yukar|WD=Q1193421|lt=yukar}}, traditional narrative songs. One day, while telling stories to his children, the father realized Payokakamu'i was outside their home. Offering a sacrifice, the deity appeared in his dreams, expressing gratitude for the offering and the stories, and revealed a method to avoid illness before leaving. When the father shared this method with the village chief, the village expressed gratitude, and the villagers were spared from the epidemic, living happily ever after.<ref name="akuma" /> Inspired by this story, rituals were performed to pray to Payokakamu'i for the end of epidemics during outbreaks.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20200418125506/https://this.kiji.is/624148580355900513 "Payokakamu'i," Stay Away: Ainu Perform Prayer Rituals] - {{ill|Kyodo News|ja|共同通信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyodo News|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 共同通信社|1=Kyodo News|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q779730}}</ref>
A mysterious figure called '''Kasunte''' was said to have been born between Payokakamu'i and a human. Kasunte repeatedly reincarnated after being killed, but the Ainu of {{ill|Akkeshi District, Hokkaido|en|Akkeshi District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1134084|lt=Akkeshi District}} finally ended this cycle by separating its upper and lower jaws. However, many of these Ainu succumbed to smallpox, and those who survived were later killed by a {{ill|Tsunami|en|Tsunami|WD=Q8070|lt=tsunami}}. This led to the belief that the tsunami was Kasunte's curse.<ref>妖怪事典, p. 105.</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * {{ill|Mintuci|en|Mintuci|WD=Q11343011|lt=Mintuci}}
{{translated page|ja|パヨカカムイ|version=98493717|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Kasunte repeatedly reincarnated after being killed, but the Ainu of {{ill|Akkeshi District, Hokkaido|en|Akkeshi District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1134084|lt=Akkeshi District}} finally ended this cycle by separating its upper and lower jaws. This led to the belief that the tsunami was Kasunte's curse.<ref>妖怪事典, p. 105.</ref>

<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ko:파요카카무이]] {{wikidata link|Q11280189}} [[Category:Ainu kamuy]] [[Category:Ainu mythology]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Smallpox]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:パヨカカムイ]][[ko:파요카카무이]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:パヨカカムイ]][[ko:파요카카무이]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:パヨカカムイ]][[ko:파요카카무이]]
'''Payokakamu'i''' (パヨカカムイ) or '''Paikaikamu'i''' (パイカイカムイ) is a {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=Deity|1=Deity}} of {{ill|epidemics|ja|疫病|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/epidemics|1=ekibyou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17239211}} in {{ill|Ainu people|en|Ainu people|WD=Q101828|lt=Ainu mythology|1=Ainu people}}.<ref name="murakami">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Kenji Murakami|ja|村上健司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenji Murakami|1=Kenji Murakami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11522709}} | title=妖怪事典 | publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]] | year=2000 | pages=267-268 | isbn=978-4-6203-1428-0}}</ref>
Unlike most Ainu deities, Payokakamu'i is known for bringing harm to people.<ref name="akuma">{{Cite book | author={{ill|Atsushi Yamakita|ja|山北篤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atsushi Yamakita|1=Atsushi Yamakita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11466547}}, supervised by Sato Toshiyuki | title=悪魔事典 | publisher=[[Shinkigensha]] | series=Truth In Fantasy | year=2000 | pages=232-233 | isbn=978-4-88317-353-2}}</ref> The deity governs {{ill|Smallpox|en|Smallpox|WD=Q12214|lt=smallpox|1=Smallpox}} and other contagious diseases. It is said to shoot invisible arrows, and those who hear the sound of these arrows are afflicted with smallpox.<ref name="murakami" />
According to one tale, a poor Ainu family lived on the outskirts of a village. The father was a poor hunter but a talented storyteller of {{ill|Yukar|en|Yukar|WD=Q1193421|lt=yukar|1=Yukar}}, traditional narrative songs. One day, while telling stories to his children, the father realized Payokakamu'i was outside their home. Offering a sacrifice, the deity appeared in his dreams, expressing gratitude for the offering and the stories, and revealed a method to avoid illness before leaving. When the father shared this method with the village chief, the village expressed gratitude, and the villagers were spared from the epidemic, living happily ever after.<ref name="akuma" /> Inspired by this story, rituals were performed to pray to Payokakamu'i for the end of epidemics during outbreaks.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20200418125506/https://this.kiji.is/624148580355900513 "Payokakamu'i," Stay Away: Ainu Perform Prayer Rituals] - {{ill|Kyodo News|ja|共同通信|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyodo News|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 共同通信社|1=Kyodo News|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q779730}}</ref>
A mysterious figure called '''Kasunte''' was said to have been born between Payokakamu'i and a human. Kasunte repeatedly reincarnated after being killed, but the Ainu of {{ill|Akkeshi District, Hokkaido|en|Akkeshi District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1134084|lt=Akkeshi District|1=Akkeshi District, Hokkaido}} finally ended this cycle by separating its upper and lower jaws. However, many of these Ainu succumbed to smallpox, and those who survived were later killed by a {{ill|Tsunami|en|Tsunami|WD=Q8070|lt=tsunami|1=Tsunami}}. This led to the belief that the tsunami was Kasunte's curse.<ref>妖怪事典, p. 105.</ref>
